**Emanuel Shinwell, Baron Shinwell**, [CH](/wiki/Companion_of_Honour "Companion of Honour"), [PC](/wiki/Privy_Council_(United_Kingdom) "Privy Council (United Kingdom)") (18 October 1884 – 8 May 1986) was a British politician who served as a government minister under [Ramsay MacDonald](/wiki/Ramsay_MacDonald "Ramsay MacDonald") and [Clement Attlee](/wiki/Clement_Attlee "Clement Attlee"). A member of the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), he served as a [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) for 40 years, representing [Linlithgowshire](/wiki/Linlithgowshire_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Linlithgowshire (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Seaham](/wiki/Seaham_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Seaham (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Easington](/wiki/Easington_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Easington (UK Parliament constituency)").

Born in the [East End of London](/wiki/East_End_of_London "East End of London") to a large family of [Jewish](/wiki/British_Jews "British Jews") immigrants, Shinwell moved to [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") as a boy and left school at the age of eleven. He became a [trade union](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") organiser and one of the leading figures of [Red Clydeside](/wiki/Red_Clydeside "Red Clydeside"). He was imprisoned in 1919 for his alleged involvement in the [disturbances in Glasgow](/wiki/Battle_of_George_Square "Battle of George Square") in January of that year. He served as a Labour MP from [1922](/wiki/1922_United_Kingdom_general_election "1922 United Kingdom general election") to [1924](/wiki/1924_United_Kingdom_general_election "1924 United Kingdom general election"), and from a by-election in 1928 until [1931](/wiki/1931_United_Kingdom_general_election "1931 United Kingdom general election"), and held junior office in the minority Labour Governments of 1924 and 1929–1931. He returned to the House of Commons in [1935](/wiki/1935_United_Kingdom_general_election "1935 United Kingdom general election"), defeating former [UK Prime Minister](/wiki/UK_Prime_Minister "UK Prime Minister") [Ramsay MacDonald](/wiki/Ramsay_MacDonald "Ramsay MacDonald"), who by that time had been expelled from the Labour Party. During the Second World War, he was a leading backbencher critic of the Coalition Government.

Shinwell is perhaps best remembered as the [Minister of Fuel and Power](/wiki/Minister_of_Fuel_and_Power "Minister of Fuel and Power") in the [Attlee ministry](/wiki/Attlee_ministry "Attlee ministry") that nationalised [coal mining in 1946](/wiki/Coal_Industry_Nationalisation_Act_1946 "Coal Industry Nationalisation Act 1946"). He was in charge of Britain's coal supply during the [extremely harsh winter of January to March 1947](/wiki/Winter_of_1946%E2%80%9347_in_the_United_Kingdom "Winter of 1946–47 in the United Kingdom"), during which the supply system collapsed, leaving the United Kingdom to freeze and close down. He became unpopular with the public and was sacked in October 1947. He then served as [Secretary of State for War](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_War "Secretary of State for War"), and then as [Minister of Defence](/wiki/Minister_of_Defence_(United_Kingdom) "Minister of Defence (United Kingdom)") from 1950 to 1951. The high defence spending which he demanded, partly to pay for British involvement in the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), was a major factor causing then-[Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer") [Hugh Gaitskell](/wiki/Hugh_Gaitskell "Hugh Gaitskell") to impose [NHS](/wiki/NHS "NHS") charges, prompting the resignation of [Aneurin Bevan](/wiki/Aneurin_Bevan "Aneurin Bevan") from the Cabinet.

Following Labour's defeat in [1951](/wiki/1951_United_Kingdom_general_election "1951 United Kingdom general election"), Shinwell continued to serve in the Shadow Cabinet in Opposition until he stepped down in 1955. Thereafter he was a senior backbencher until [1970](/wiki/1970_United_Kingdom_general_election "1970 United Kingdom general election"), by which time he was in his mid-eighties. That year he accepted a life peerage and was an active member of the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") until shortly before his death, aged 101, in 1986.

## Early life, career and trade union activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manny_Shinwell&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life, career and trade union activities")]

Shinwell was born in [Spitalfields](/wiki/Spitalfields "Spitalfields"), London, but his family moved to [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), Scotland. His father was a Polish [Jew](/wiki/Jew "Jew") who had a small clothing shop, and his mother, a Dutch Jew, was a cook from London.[[1]](#cite_note-1) He was the eldest of thirteen children.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

He educated himself in a public library and at the [Kelvingrove Art Gallery](/wiki/Kelvingrove_Art_Gallery "Kelvingrove Art Gallery"). He enjoyed sport, particularly boxing, and he was the trainer of a local football team. He left school at age eleven to be apprenticed as a tailor, and began his working life as a machinist in a clothing workshop. In 1903, he became active in the Amalgamated Union of Clothiers' Operatives, and joined the [Glasgow Trades Council](/wiki/Glasgow_Trades_Council "Glasgow Trades Council") in 1906 as a delegate of that union.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

In May 1911, he was seconded to help organise the seamen of Glasgow at the request of [Havelock Wilson](/wiki/Havelock_Wilson "Havelock Wilson") of the [National Sailors' and Firemen's Union](/wiki/National_Sailors%27_and_Firemen%27s_Union "National Sailors' and Firemen's Union") (NSFU). He played a prominent role in the six-week Glasgow seamen's [strike](/wiki/Strike_action "Strike action") which began on 14 June and which was part of a nationwide strike. He subsequently became the secretary of the Glasgow branch of the NSFU. In August 1912, he participated in a revolt against the union, which resulted in the Glasgow branch becoming part of the [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton")-based [British Seafarers' Union](/wiki/British_Seafarers%27_Union "British Seafarers' Union") (BSU). He was the local secretary of the BSU until it became part of the [Amalgamated Marine Workers' Union](/wiki/Amalgamated_Marine_Workers%27_Union "Amalgamated Marine Workers' Union") (AMWU) in 1922, after which he served as National Organiser of the new organisation.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Emanuel_Shinwell%2C_1918.jpg/250px-Emanuel_Shinwell%2C_1918.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emanuel_Shinwell,_1918.jpg)

Shinwell (left) at an election meeting in 1918

In [1918](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election "1918 United Kingdom general election"), he stood unsuccessfully for [Linlithgowshire](/wiki/Linlithgowshire_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Linlithgowshire (UK Parliament constituency)")[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2) (alternative name West Lothian[[3]](#cite_note-3)).

In 1919, he gained national notoriety through his involvement in the [Glasgow 40 Hours' Movement](/wiki/Red_Clydeside "Red Clydeside"). This movement culminated in clashes between police and protesters in Glasgow's [George Square](/wiki/George_Square "George Square") in January 1919, in which he was alleged to have been involved. He was afterwards tried for incitement to riot and was sentenced to five months' imprisonment in [Calton Jail](/wiki/Calton_Jail "Calton Jail"), [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

## Political career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manny_Shinwell&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Political career")]

### Interwar and World War Two

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manny_Shinwell&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Interwar and World War Two")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/3c/Manny_Shinwell_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manny_Shinwell_1.jpg)

Shinwell in 1920

An [Independent Labour Party](/wiki/Independent_Labour_Party "Independent Labour Party") (ILP) member, he was elected as [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) for [Linlithgowshire](/wiki/Linlithgowshire_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Linlithgowshire (UK Parliament constituency)") at the [1922 general election](/wiki/1922_United_Kingdom_general_election "1922 United Kingdom general election").[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

In 1924 he was [Secretary for Mines](/wiki/Secretary_for_Mines "Secretary for Mines") (not a Cabinet-level post)[[5]](#cite_note-5) in the First Labour Government. He lost his seat in [1924](/wiki/1924_United_Kingdom_general_election "1924 United Kingdom general election"), but was re-elected for Linlithgowshire at a [by-election](/wiki/By-election "By-election") in 1928.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

In the second Labour Government of 1929–31 [Ramsay MacDonald](/wiki/Ramsay_MacDonald "Ramsay MacDonald") appointed him [Financial Secretary to the War Office](/wiki/Financial_Secretary_to_the_War_Office "Financial Secretary to the War Office") (1929–30); Cowling says that MacDonald believed he had rescued Shinwell's ministerial career when no minister would take him. He then served again as [Secretary for Mines](/wiki/Secretary_for_Mines "Secretary for Mines") from 1930 to 1931. At the time Shinwell was an admirer of MacDonald and tried to dissuade him from forming a [National Government](/wiki/National_Government_(1931%E2%80%931935) "National Government (1931–1935)") in 1931. He again lost his seat at the [general election that year](/wiki/1931_United_Kingdom_general_election "1931 United Kingdom general election").[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

He returned to the Commons in [1935](/wiki/1935_United_Kingdom_general_election "1935 United Kingdom general election") after defeating MacDonald for [Seaham Harbour](/wiki/Seaham_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Seaham (UK Parliament constituency)"), [County of Durham](/wiki/County_of_Durham "County of Durham") (later renamed Easington after boundary changes in the late 1940s).[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

He campaigned vigorously, along with left-wingers such as [Aneurin Bevan](/wiki/Aneurin_Bevan "Aneurin Bevan"), for Britain to support the [Popular Front](/wiki/Popular_Front_(Spain) "Popular Front (Spain)") government in Spain against [Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco") in the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"). On 4 April 1938, he doggedly criticised the government's foreign policy during a heated [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") debate. Eventually, [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") MP [Robert Bower](/wiki/Robert_Bower_(Conservative_politician) "Robert Bower (Conservative politician)") became exasperated and shouted at him to "[go back to Poland](/wiki/Go_back_to_Poland "Go back to Poland")!".[[6]](#cite_note-6) Shinwell took this to be an [anti-semitic](/wiki/Anti-semitic "Anti-semitic") remark and struck Bower on the side of the head causing internal bleeding, a blood clot, and a burst left eardrum.[[7]](#cite_note-time1938-7) Both men then apologized to each other.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In May 1940 Shinwell refused a position in [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill")'s [Coalition Government](/wiki/Coalition_Government "Coalition Government") in the [Ministry of Food](/wiki/Minister_of_Food_(United_Kingdom) "Minister of Food (United Kingdom)").[[9]](#cite_note-9) He thought the offer of the Food post a “bloody insult”.[[10]](#cite_note-10) He became chairman of the Labour Party in 1942. During the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") he was a vigorous but patriotic backbench critic of Churchill. He and [Earl Winterton](/wiki/Edward_Turnour,_6th_Earl_Winterton "Edward Turnour, 6th Earl Winterton"), another serial critic of the government, were known as "[Arsenic and Old Lace](/wiki/Arsenic_and_Old_Lace_(play) "Arsenic and Old Lace (play)")".[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Attlee Governments: 1945–51

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manny_Shinwell&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Attlee Governments: 1945–51")]

#### Minister of Fuel and Power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manny_Shinwell&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Minister of Fuel and Power")]

He served in [Clement Attlee](/wiki/Clement_Attlee "Clement Attlee")'s Cabinet after the Labour victory in [1945](/wiki/1945_United_Kingdom_general_election "1945 United Kingdom general election") as Minister of Fuel and Power, and in 1946 he presided over the [nationalisation of the mining industry](/wiki/Coal_Industry_Nationalisation_Act_1946 "Coal Industry Nationalisation Act 1946"). He also negotiated a miners' charter with the NUM. He declared the middle class "not worth a tinker's cuss".[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2) His insistence on the open-cast mining of the park of the [Wentworth Woodhouse](/wiki/Wentworth_Woodhouse "Wentworth Woodhouse") estate, to the doorsteps of the house, when the quality of the coal was poor, was viewed by its owners and the local mining community, which opposed it, as pure vindictiveness – an act of class warfare.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 1947-8 he was Chairman of the Labour Party.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

In 1947, Britain experienced, in an [exceptionally severe winter](/wiki/Winter_of_1946%E2%80%9347_in_the_United_Kingdom "Winter of 1946–47 in the United Kingdom"), a serious coal shortage. The supply system collapsed, leaving Britain to freeze and close down. Shinwell denied there were problems and refused to assume responsibility, blaming the climate, the railway system, or capitalism generally.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Shinwell was widely criticised for his failure to avert this crisis. His earlier comment that "There will be no fuel crisis. I am Minister of Fuel and Power and I ought to know", was later included in the official handbook for Conservative Party members to use in speeches and leaflets.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In 1947 Shinwell presided over the [nationalisation of electricity](/wiki/Electricity_Act_1947 "Electricity Act 1947"). In October 1947 he was sacked. He was bitterly resentful at being replaced by [Hugh Gaitskell](/wiki/Hugh_Gaitskell "Hugh Gaitskell"), his former deputy and a public schoolboy. He was also attacked by [James Callaghan](/wiki/James_Callaghan "James Callaghan") (then a junior minister) for his lack of zeal about further nationalisation.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

#### Secretary of State for War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manny_Shinwell&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Secretary of State for War")]

Shinwell was demoted to [Secretary of State for War](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_War "Secretary of State for War") (Minister for the Army, but no longer a full member of the Cabinet) a position which he held until 1950. He was a vigorous War Minister, who got on well with the Army and was seen as jingoistic.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

In November 1947 a report from [MI5](/wiki/MI5 "MI5") alleged that Shinwell had passed secret information to a man named "Stanley", who had passed it on to [Zionist](/wiki/Zionist "Zionist") [paramilitary](/wiki/Paramilitary "Paramilitary") group, the [Irgun](/wiki/Irgun "Irgun"). Shinwell knew self-styled "contact man" [Sidney Stanley](/wiki/Sidney_Stanley "Sidney Stanley"), whom he had approached for help in finding employment for his son Ernie, and Stanley had obtained information on the disbandment of the [Transjordan Frontier Force](/wiki/Transjordan_Frontier_Force "Transjordan Frontier Force") from some government source.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

#### Minister of Defence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manny_Shinwell&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Minister of Defence")]

Shinwell's seat became [Easington](/wiki/Easington_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Easington (UK Parliament constituency)") at the [February 1950 election](/wiki/1950_United_Kingdom_general_election "1950 United Kingdom general election"), after which he was promoted to [Minister of Defence](/wiki/Minister_of_Defence_(United_Kingdom) "Minister of Defence (United Kingdom)") and became a full member of the Cabinet once more. [Edmund Dell](/wiki/Edmund_Dell "Edmund Dell") described him as "putty in the hands of the defence chiefs" and his promotion as "[a] ludicrous appointment. No failure was ever great enough to persuade Attlee to deny one of his cohorts new opportunities to do damage … Shinwell never forgave Gaitskell, whom he blamed for his disgrace." Gaitskell, promoted to [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer") later in the year, recorded in his diary that Shinwell "never loses an opportunity of picking a quarrel with me, sometimes on the most ridiculous grounds".[[16]](#cite_note-auto-16)

His term of office saw the [Malayan Emergency](/wiki/Malayan_Emergency "Malayan Emergency") and the early stages of [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), which began in June 1950 and to which [British troops were deployed](/wiki/1st_Commonwealth_Division "1st Commonwealth Division").[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2) Shinwell was responsible for the rearmament programme which precipitated the resignation of [Aneurin Bevan](/wiki/Aneurin_Bevan "Aneurin Bevan") from the Cabinet in the spring of 1951, although Gaitskell actually gave him less defence spending than he wanted.[[16]](#cite_note-auto-16) In the summer of 1951 the Cabinet blocked him from sending British troops to [Abadan](/wiki/Abadan "Abadan") when the [oil refineries were nationalised](/wiki/Abadan_Crisis "Abadan Crisis") by the Iranian government.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

Shinwell was by now seen as being on the right of the Labour Party. At the Labour Party Conference at Scarborough that autumn, he lost his place as an elected constituency representative on the Labour Party National Executive Committee (NEC), the members of which were increasingly elected by Bevanites in the constituency parties. Labour lost the [general election a month later](/wiki/1951_United_Kingdom_general_election "1951 United Kingdom general election").[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Later political career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manny_Shinwell&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Later political career")]

Shinwell stepped down from the Shadow Cabinet (which at that time was elected by Labour MPs when the party was in opposition) in 1955. That year he published a volume of memoirs, *Conflict Without Malice*.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2) By the early 1960s he had changed his mind about nuclear weapons and opposed the deployment of US nuclear submarines to Holy Loch.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

Shinwell did not resume ministerial office when Labour returned to power in October 1964, but instead the new Prime Minister [Harold Wilson](/wiki/Harold_Wilson "Harold Wilson") appointed him Chairman of the [Parliamentary Labour Party](/wiki/Parliamentary_Labour_Party "Parliamentary Labour Party"), and during the 1964-6 Parliament he worked hard to drum up backbench support for the government, which had a very narrow majority.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2) He was appointed to the [Order of the Companions of Honour](/wiki/Order_of_the_Companions_of_Honour "Order of the Companions of Honour") in the [1965 Birthday Honours](/wiki/1965_Birthday_Honours "1965 Birthday Honours").[[19]](#cite_note-19) He was vehemently opposed to Wilson's attempt to enter the EEC in 1966, and resigned as Chairman of the Labour Party in 1967.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

He got on well both with Field Marshal [Bernard Montgomery](/wiki/Bernard_Montgomery "Bernard Montgomery") and with the journalist Sir [John Junor](/wiki/John_Junor "John Junor").[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

Shinwell was created a [life peer](/wiki/Life_peer "Life peer") as **Baron Shinwell**, of [Easington](/wiki/Easington,_County_Durham "Easington, County Durham") in the [County of Durham](/wiki/County_of_Durham "County of Durham"), on 29 June 1970.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) He later became chair of the All-Party Lords Defence Study Group. In 1973 he published another volume of memoirs, *I've Lived through It All*. He voted against the Labour Government in 1976. He resigned the Labour Party whip in 1982 in protest at left wing militancy.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manny_Shinwell&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Death")]

In October 1984 Shinwell celebrated his hundredth birthday against the backdrop of the [miners' strike](/wiki/UK_miners%27_strike_(1984%E2%80%9385) "UK miners' strike (1984–85)").[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2) He continued to be active in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") until shortly before his death.[[22]](#cite_note-22) He became the longest-lived [peer](/wiki/Peerage "Peerage") on 26 March 1986, dying a little over a month later on 8 May, aged 101. He held the record for the second longest-lived British MP (after [Theodore Cooke Taylor](/wiki/Theodore_Cooke_Taylor "Theodore Cooke Taylor")) until overtaken by [Bert Hazell](/wiki/Bert_Hazell "Bert Hazell") in November 2008.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2) He is one of nine former UK MPs to have become a centenarian.

Shinwell's estate was valued for probate at £271,509 (around £800,000 at 2023 prices).[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manny_Shinwell&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Shinwell was married three times: from 1903 to 1954 to Fay (Fanny) Freeman, by whom he had two sons and a daughter, from 1956 to 1971 to Dinah Meyer, who was [Danish](/wiki/Danes "Danes"), and from 1972 to 1977 to Sarah Sturgo.[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2) He outlived all three of his wives.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Shinwell's great-niece is the former MP for [Liverpool Wavertree](/wiki/Liverpool_Wavertree "Liverpool Wavertree"), [Luciana Berger](/wiki/Luciana_Berger "Luciana Berger").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Shinwell sat for sculptor [Alan Thornhill](/wiki/Alan_Thornhill "Alan Thornhill") for a portrait[[26]](#cite_note-26) in clay. The correspondence file relating to the Shinwell portrait bust is held as part of the Thornhill Papers (2006:56) in the archive[[27]](#cite_note-27) of the [Henry Moore Foundation](/wiki/Henry_Moore_Foundation "Henry Moore Foundation")'s [Henry Moore Institute](/wiki/Henry_Moore_Institute "Henry Moore Institute") in [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds") and the terracotta remains in the collection of the artist. A bronze (accession number S.309) was purchased for the Collection of Glasgow City Art Gallery in 1973.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Shinwell was a lifelong smoker, having smoked [tobacco](/wiki/Tobacco "Tobacco") from the age of 13; on many occasions he was seen smoking with his pipe. Shinwell was also an enthusiastic drinker of [spirits](/wiki/Liquor "Liquor"), particularly [whisky](/wiki/Whisky "Whisky").[[29]](#cite_note-29)
